description: Maps every area of the repository to the code it owns and the documentation that governs it, so 'which docs govern this change?' always has a machine-readable answer. This file is committed data guarded by an admission test: scripts/check-area-map.js fails CI when the map goes stale (a pattern matching no tracked file, an entry pointing at an untracked file), when a tracked file resolves to no area and is neither a repo-wide doc nor a justified exception, or when any tracked .md doc (anywhere in the repo, not only under docs/) is placed in no area's doc set, repo-wide list, or justified 'unassigned' exception — being matched only by a code pattern is not a doc home. An area owns code, a doc set, or both: a docs-only area (no code) is valid when it carries a non-empty doc set. Code patterns support '*', '**', and {a,b} alternation only, and every brace alternative must match at least one tracked file. Doc entries are literal tracked paths, never patterns. Code files and docs are many-to-many: a file or doc may belong to several areas. A code file may also name a governing doc directly with a '// see docs/<path>.md' comment, which adds every area whose doc set contains that doc. A file may instead DECLARE its complete governing doc set with a 'declared-governance' entry (a required 'governed-by' array — [] states the governing set is empty): that declared set governs the file's disposition in place of the docs its covering code area would supply, and area membership and '// see' pointers add nothing more. A declared file keeps the code-area coverage its patterns give it (a declaration grants no coverage of its own), and its governance is declared once and from one source (a declared file is not also a repo-wide doc, nor does it carry a '// see' pointer into a doc set).

repo-wide.description: Docs that apply to every area of the repository — a change anywhere can be governed by them. They resolve in their own right and do not need to belong to an area's doc set (though some also do).

unassigned.reason: License text — not project documentation and owned by no area.
Contributor license agreement — project governance, not area documentation.
Shipped end-user assets (icons, the reading guide delivered with dispatch payloads) — product artifacts, not project documentation.

Security policy — coordinated-disclosure governance (how a vulnerability is reported and fixed), referenced from .github/CONTRIBUTING.md; a process/governance doc, not code-governing area documentation.
Code of Conduct (Contributor Covenant) — community governance, not area documentation.
Pull-request body template — contribution scaffolding whose rules live in .github/CONTRIBUTING.md; a form, not area documentation.
Reference-implementations index — orientation for runnable example artifacts (never shipped in a release), not project doctrine.
Reference sync-server README — implementation notes for a runnable example artifact (never shipped); the protocol it implements is governed by docs/api/sync-protocol.md.

declared-governance.reason: Registered-clause verification data — records keyed to clause ids, consumed by the docs-disposition check and validated by scripts/check-clause-registry.js.
Unit tests for the CI-machinery check scripts — they exercise the check tooling itself; their contract is the behaviour of the scripts under test.
cargo-mutants configuration — the desktop crate's Rust mutation-run settings.
Codecov configuration — coverage-report and status settings.
Stryker configuration — JavaScript mutation-testing settings.
The shared-code copy mechanism — its copy model, outputs, and freshness rule are owned by the shared-core architecture doc.
The shared-views injection — the one-fragment-two-shells assembly the shared-core architecture doc owns.
Format-subject unit suites — they exercise the shared format modules and schema tooling; their contract is the format doctrine, not the sync protocol their tree-mates ride.
The backward-compatibility harness — the compat doc owns its discovery and baseline rules, and the format doc's stamp-relaxation clause cites it.
The sufficiency baseline lock — the lint doc owns the both-direction locking and regeneration rules it enforces.
The conformance-vector locks — they guard clauses of the locator procedure, the corpus doctrine, and the format doc's measurement clause.
The no-shipped-resolver guard — it enforces the locator doc's never-an-implementation rule and the corpus doctrine's inert-vector posture.
The vector measurement modules — they implement the locator doc's per-strategy query semantics for the harnesses.
The comparator's unit suite — the corpus doctrine owns the comparison classes and refusal rules it pins.
Unit tests for CI machinery plus the schema-artifact size budget — no doc states the budget or the tooling contracts, so the governing set is empty.
UI-subject unit suites — they exercise the shared panel fragment's rendering and the conflict-view module; their contract is the panel-ui doctrine, not the sync/format union their tree ride supplies.

Sync chokepoints implementing the format's step-history and field-shape rules — its committed format pointer would, after the schema-contract split, drag that area's locator and taxonomy docs onto a module that emits no locators; this declaration states its true governors: its areas' docs plus the format doc the pointer names.

The conflict-view module — both a sync-state consumer and the panel fragment's resolution view — its committed format pointer would, after the schema-contract split, drag that area's locator and taxonomy docs onto a module that emits no locators; this declaration states its true governors: its areas' docs plus the format doc the pointer names.

The shared rendering module — resolves and renders the committed step history for the panel fragment — its committed format pointer would, after the schema-contract split, drag that area's locator and taxonomy docs onto a module that emits no locators; this declaration states its true governors: its areas' docs plus the format doc the pointer names.
The stamp-gate suite — it pins the sync protocol's skip-and-report mismatch handling over the format's version stamp, so both doctrines govern it.
The composition suite — beyond the format doctrine it is the registry-cited guard for locator, extension-capture, and sufficiency clauses over the composed schemas.

The extension panel — its committed format pointer would drag the schema-contract set onto a module that emits no locators; this declaration states its true governors: the sidepanel area's docs plus the format doc its commit logic implements.

The CI hub workflow — beyond the CI guides it implements the shared-core freshness gate, the Rust test self-classification, the coverage staging topology, the release process's platform-scoped call and secret contracts, and the corpus doctrine's producer/differ job topology and pinned-image rule.
The weekly mutation workflow — the mutation-strategy doc owns its cadence and threshold posture.
The release-output guard — the feature-branch dist gate that the format doc's version-bump clause is verified by, per its registry row and the release process doc.

Committed release-output copies — regenerated by the release pipeline, hard-blocked in feature PRs by the release-output guard, and carried by the automation PR whose generated body has no disposition sections.
